canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, DAN is used to assess the safety of large language models in the context of jailbreaking. There is a slight variation in its framing: some work describes it as a "benchmark collection of jailbreak-enhanced instructions," while other work characterizes it more specifically as a "well-known jailbreaking prompt" named "Do Anything Now." Papers use DAN to evaluate a model's "resilience against diverse jailbreak attacks" and to measure "Backdoor vulnerability." One study operationalizes this by employing a specific version, DAN 13.0, as a baseline method intended to "override AI safety restrictions and elicit biased responses." This positions DAN as an instrument for both attempting to bypass safety features and for benchmarking a model's robustness against such attempts.
papers: ChineseSafetyQA: A Safety Short-form Factuality Benchmark for Large Language Models
Marco-Bench-MIF: On Multilingual Instruction-Following Capability of Large Language
BabyLM’s First Constructions: Causal interventions provide a signal of learning
Large Language Models for Automated Literature Review: An Evaluation of Reference Generation, Abstract Writing, and Review Composition

definitions.text: A benchmark collection of jailbreak-enhanced instructions used to evaluate a model's resilience against diverse jailbreak attacks.
definitions.paper: ChineseSafetyQA: A Safety Short-form Factuality Benchmark for Large Language Models

relationships.paper: Watch Out for Your Agents! Investigating Backdoor Threats to LLM-Based Agents
